items.scenario: You call an LLM SDK with max_tokens="512" (a string) instead of 512 (an int).
items.question: What is likely to happen, and how do you fix it?
items.answer: The SDK either raises a validation/TypeError or the API rejects the request, because max_tokens must be an integer. Strong typing means the string is not silently converted. Fix it by passing the int 512, or convert explicitly with int("512") when the value comes from text input or an environment variable.

items.scenario: Two variables reference the same message-history list. You append a turn to one and the other unexpectedly changes too.
items.question: Why does this happen and how do you prevent it?
items.answer: Lists are mutable and assignment copies the reference, not the list, so both names point to one object. To keep an independent copy use history.copy() for a flat list, or copy.deepcopy(history) when the messages contain nested mutable structures.

items.scenario: A helper def build(history=[]) keeps accumulating messages across separate calls even though you never pass history.
items.question: What is the cause and the correct pattern?
items.answer: The default argument [] is evaluated once at function definition and reused on every call, so it grows over time. Use history=None and create a new list inside: history = history if history is not None else [].
